items.front: Under 2-2-1, what goes in box 26 when a flight plan is received by voice recorder rather than written on a strip?
items.back: The sector or position number to identify where the recording may be found if SAR activities become necessary.

items.front: Under 2-2-1, how do military overseas flights normally file IFR flight plans?
items.back: Through a specified military base operations office (BASOPS), which forwards the IFR flight notification to the appropriate center. Pilots file directly with FAA only if BASOPS is not available.

items.front: Under 2-2-2, what must be recorded on the form when forwarding flight plan information?
items.back: The time of filing and delivery. During EAS FDP operation, these are recorded automatically for interfaced facilities.

items.front: Under 2-2-2, when are manual forwarding actions required during EAS FDP operation?
items.back: Only when the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer.

items.front: Under 2-2-2, where must proposed tower en route flight plans be forwarded?
items.back: To the appropriate departure terminal facility.

items.front: Under 2-2-3, when must terminal facilities forward VFR departure times?
items.back: Only when FSSs or military operations offices have requested them. Other VFR flight plan data is forwarded only if requested by the pilot.

items.front: Under 2-2-4, what DVFR data must be forwarded from joint-use airports and to whom?
items.back: Departure times on all DVFR departures must be forwarded to the military operations office.

items.front: Under 2-2-4, what phrase must civil pilots departing DVFR from a joint-use airport include in their initial call-up?
items.back: 'DVFR to (destination)' in the initial call-up to an FAA-operated tower.

items.front: Under 2-2-5, what must a controller do when a pilot wants to change from IFR to VFR?
items.back: Request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS. The controller does not process the change directly.

items.front: Under 2-2-6, list the three conditions that allow modification of the 15-minute forwarding time requirement.
items.back: 1. LOA/facility directive can REDUCE the time of subpara a. 2. Automated systems or mandatory radar handoffs can INCREASE subpara b1 and 2-2-11a time up to 15 min. 3. Manual data processing or nonradar operations can INCREASE subpara a time.

items.front: Under 2-2-6a, which IFR flight progress data item is NOT required for military or scheduled air carrier aircraft?
items.back: Item 9: ETA at destination airport.

items.front: Under 2-2-6b, when must a position report over the last reporting point be forwarded?
items.back: 1. Time differs more than 3 minutes from estimate given. 2. Requested by receiving facility. 3. Agreed to between facilities.

items.front: Under 2-2-6a item 12, what nonradar separation triggers mandatory inclusion of longitudinal separation information?
items.back: When aircraft at the same altitude have less than 10 minutes separation at the facilities' boundary, unless otherwise specified in an LOA.

items.front: Under 2-2-7, what must you do with a computer-assigned beacon code when manually entering a flight plan?
items.back: Insert the beacon code in the appropriate field as part of the input message.

items.front: Under 2-2-8, what items are forwarded for an approved ALTRV flight?
items.back: Only items necessary to: identify the flight, update ALTRV APVL data, or revise previously given information. The full 2-2-6a list does not apply.

items.front: Under 2-2-9, what service is used to verify computer message receipt, and what three data items are verified?
items.back: Service F. The three items: 1. Aircraft identification. 2. Assigned altitude. 3. Departure or coordination fix time. Also verify any IFR/EAS VFR cancellation (subpara b).

items.front: Under 2-2-9, when is Service F verification NOT required?
items.back: When your facility is equipped to automatically obtain acknowledgment of receipt of transferred data.

items.front: Under 2-2-10, contrast when Computer B network vs. NADIN is used to transmit proposed flight plans.
items.back: Computer B: during inter-center computer operation for flights within PBCT parameter. NADIN: when route exceeds 20 elements external to originating ARTCC (even during computer ops), OR during nonautomated operation when any of three conditions met.

items.front: Under 2-2-10b, what three conditions require NADIN forwarding during nonautomated operation?
items.back: 1. 10+ elements external to originating center AND 3+ other center areas. 2. 10+ elements primarily fix-radial-distance or lat/long format, regardless of centers. 3. Flight plan remarks too lengthy for interphone transmission.

items.front: Under 2-2-11a, what is the ETA revision threshold?
items.back: ETA revisions need only be forwarded when the time differs by more than 3 minutes from the estimate given.

items.front: Under 2-2-11c, what dual coordination is required for amendments within 30 minutes of proposed departure?
items.back: Verbal AND automated coordination with the receiving facility. If automated means are unavailable, verbal coordination is sufficient.

items.front: Under 2-2-11e, what is the time limit for verifying flight plan data to FDIO-only facilities after a UTM notification?
items.back: Within three minutes of the transfer of control point estimate. FDIO-only facilities have FDIO but without STARS.

items.front: Under 2-2-12, what two categories of information from airborne military aircraft must be forwarded to FSSs?
items.back: 1. IFR flight plans and changes from VFR to IFR flight plans. 2. Changes to an IFR flight plan (destination change or fuel exhaustion time change).

items.front: Under 2-2-12, list the eight items to forward when a military aircraft changes destination.
items.back: 1. Aircraft ID & type, 2. Departure point, 3. Original destination, 4. Position & time, 5. New destination, 6. ETA, 7. Remarks including change in fuel exhaustion time, 8. Revised ETA.

items.front: Under 2-2-13, what is the standard lead time for U.S.-Canada flight plan exchanges?
items.back: 30 minutes or as bilaterally agreed (domestic). International route changes: optimum 30 minutes or as soon as available. SIDs are excepted from the international route change requirement.

items.front: Under 2-2-13c, when a flight goes U.S.→Canada→U.S., who forwards data back to the U.S. ARTCC?
items.back: The Canadian ACC, by voice transmission. Exception: flights on mutually agreed airways/fixes determined case-by-case at the service area office.

items.front: Under 2-2-14, what two formats do U.S. ARTCCs use for Canadian flight data?
items.back: 1. NADIN II input format (standard for flight plan, amendment, cancellation, and response messages). 2. Transport Canada (TC) ACC Flight Strip Format (when NADIN II data exceeds strip form field size, for fields 1-8 of 2-2-6a).

items.front: Under 2-2-14, which message type do Canadian ACCs send that U.S. ARTCCs do not?
items.back: Correction messages. U.S. sends response messages (acknowledgment, error, rejection) to Canadian input instead.

items.front: Under 2-2-15, when is 'NRP' retained vs. removed from the flight plan remarks?
items.back: RETAINED: when aircraft moved due to weather, traffic, or tactical reasons. REMOVED: when route altered due to pilot request.

items.front: Under 2-2-15, what coordination is required before entering 'NRP' in remarks?
items.back: Prior coordination with ATCSCC or as prescribed by international NRP flight operations procedures. The en route facility where an international flight requests NRP participation enters 'NRP'.
